BIHAR (Metro Rail News): Indian Railways has approved a Rs 499 crore project to double the 44.40 km Mansi-Saharsa rail line in Bihar. The project will help reduce congestion on this busy route and improve the movement of both passenger and freight trains. The Mansi-Saharsa section, which is part of East Central Railway, currently operates as a single-line route on the Mansi-Saraigarh corridor. Every day, this line handles a large number of passenger and freight trains, which makes it an important rail link in the region. The existing route already carries heavy traffic. At present around 24 pairs of passenger trains run on this section every day. The route also transports several important commodities, which includes – wheat, maize, ballast, boiled rice, cement, fertilisers, rice, salt, sand, stone and sugar. Indian Railways data also shows that this section is operating beyond its ideal capacity. Line utilization has reached 108.11% and could rise to 119.34% by 2028-29 if extra capacity is not created. The second track will allow more trains to run on this route and reduce delays caused by traffic congestion. The project will help: Once the project becomes operational, the route will be able to handle an additional 1.764 million tonnes of freight traffic each year. The added capacity will support the transportation of agricultural produce, construction materials, and other goods that move through this corridor. The Mansi-Saharsa route plays an important role in connecting people and goods across north Bihar. With passenger numbers and freight traffic expected to grow further in the coming years, Railways has moved ahead with the doubling project to strengthen the corridor and improve overall efficiency. NAGPUR (Metro Rail News): The Maharashtra government has provided an additional Rs 30 crore for Nagpur Metro Phase-2. After this the total funding rose to Rs 607 crore. This new funding comes as construction, land acquisition, and expansion plans continue to progress. The state government released these funds as part of its equity commitment under an agreement signed by the Centre, the Maharashtra government, and Maharashtra Metro Rail Corporation Limited (MahaMetro) in September 2023. Officials noted that the Urban Development Department sanctioned the latest Rs 30 crore from the state budget for 2026-27. With this latest release, Maharashtra’s total contribution to Nagpur Metro Phase-2 has reached Rs 607 crore. This amount represents over 60% of the state’s total committed share of Rs 998.20 crore. The state still needs to release the remaining Rs 391.20 crore. Nagpur Metro Phase-2 plans to add a 43.8 km metro network with 32 stations across four corridors. This recent funding comes right after Maharashtra’s cabinet infrastructure committee approved a 1.4 km extension of the Kamptee corridor beyond the Kanhan River to Kanhan town. The proposed extension: The proposal is now with the Union Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA) for final approval. MahaMetro is planning to start work after receiving clearance. Land acquisition and construction progress Land acquisition has also gained momentum. The project needed 9.04 hectares but the authorities have already acquired or processed more than half. MahaMetro has received permission to start work on nearly 80% of the needed land parcels. It also includes some defence land. Officials also indicated that they do not expect major land-related issues that would delay construction. Work has also progressed on several key structures across the corridors. The project will also provide rail access to the National Maritime Heritage Complex (NMHC) at Lothal, a major cultural and tourism project, and to the upcoming Dholera International Airport. Dholera continues to attract investments as a future industrial and economic hub. Better rail connectivity will support passenger travel, industrial development and future growth in the region. The FT global inflation and interest rates tracker provides a regularly updated picture of consumer price inflation and central bank policy rates around the world. Our tracker features the latest inflation and policy rate data for most countries, historical figures, as well as key factors affecting policymakers’ decisions on borrowing costs. articles.title: Introducing: The Story of Money
articles.raw_summary: A new video podcast from The FT exploring the history of finance
articles.extracted_text: Simply sign up to the Global Economy myFT Digest -- delivered directly to your inbox. Those who do not study history may be destined to repeat it . . . and lose money. The Story of Money is a new podcast from The Financial Times exploring the past to see where the next opportunities — and disasters — may lie. Hosted by FT columnist Gillian Tett and FT Alphaville editor Robin Wigglesworth , The Story of Money explores the history of global finance through the people, ideas, events, and institutions that have shaped it. From unregulated frontier banking experiments in 19th-century America to institutionalised debt forgiveness in ancient Mesopotamia, The Story of Money traces how financial systems evolve — and why familiar patterns often repeat — offering context for everything from market cycles to emerging AI trends. The podcast draws on the FT’s global newsroom and 138-year history of reporting, combining analysis, storytelling and insight, with contributions from a cast of FT journalists and expert guests. New episodes will be released weekly on Wednesdays from 22 April. Watch, listen and subscribe for free on YouTube , Apple Podcasts , Spotify , or wherever you get your podcasts. The Story of Money joins the FT’s network of podcasts, including the FT News Briefing , Unhedged , The Economics Show with Soumaya Keynes , Political Fix , Tech Tonic , The Rachman Review and Hot Money . Gillian Tett is a columnist and member of the FT’s editorial board. She writes a weekly column covering a range of economic, financial, political and social issues. She also serves as Provost of King’s College, Cambridge. Previously, she chaired the FT editorial board, and ran Moral Money, the FT’s sustainability newsletter which she co-founded. Gillian’s earlier roles included US managing editor for the FT, capital markets editor; deputy Lex editor, Tokyo bureau chief and reporter in Russia and Brussels. She has won Columnist of the Year (2014), Journalist of the Year (2009) and Business Journalist of the Year (2008) in the British Press Awards and three SABEW awards. She is the author of four books including Fool’s Gold , the inside story of the 2008 financial crisis. Robin Wigglesworth is the editor of Alphaville, the FT’s financial blog. From Oslo, Norway he leads a team of three other writers in London and New York that dig into anything deeply nerdy or plain delightful that they spot in markets, business or the global economy. Previously, Robin was the FT’s global finance correspondent, US markets editor, deputy head of fastFT, capital markets correspondent and Gulf correspondent. He joined the FT from Bloomberg News in 2008, where he covered Nordic economics. He is the author of Trillions , a book on the past, present and future of passive investing, and A Fabulous Debt, a history of the bond market.

articles.title: Federal Reserve issues initial findings from its 2025 triennial payments study
articles.raw_summary: Federal Reserve issues initial findings from its 2025 triennial payments study
articles.extracted_text: An official website of the United States Government Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you've safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. The Federal Reserve, the central bank of the United States, provides the nation with a safe, flexible, and stable monetary and financial system. The Federal Reserve on Wednesday issued initial findings from its 2025 triennial payments study. The information shows how consumers and businesses chose to make noncash payments, using different types of cards, ACH payments, and checks. The study shows that the total number of noncash payments made by consumers and businesses increased to 236.6 billion in 2024. Overall, the number of noncash payments more than tripled since 2000. Cards once again were used most frequently, accounting for over three quarters of payments by number. Debit cards continued to account for the majority of all card payments, although credit card payments grew faster than debit card payments for the first time in almost a decade. Payments using the ACH system continued to account for the majority of noncash payments by value. In 2024, ACH's share of noncash payments by value reached almost three quarters for the first time. The data also show that check payments and ATM cash withdrawals continued to decline by both number and value. The Federal Reserve Payments Study is a collaborative effort of the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta and the Federal Reserve Board. The triennial study has been conducted every three years since 2001 with annual supplements since 2017. The study develops aggregate estimates using data collected from voluntary surveys of depository institutions, card networks, and other major payment processors. Additional details will be made available as more analysis is completed. For media inquiries, please email [email protected] or call 202-452-2955.

The Federal Reserve, the central bank of the United States, provides the nation with a safe, flexible, and stable monetary and financial system. Federal bank regulatory agencies today released the 2026 list of certain geographies where certain bank activities are eligible for Community Reinvestment Act (CRA) credit. Under the CRA, the agencies assess a bank's record of meeting the credit needs of its entire community, including low- and moderate-income neighborhoods, consistent with safe and sound operations. The list released by the agencies includes distressed or underserved nonmetropolitan middle-income geographies where revitalization or stabilization activities are eligible to receive CRA consideration. The designations reflect local economic conditions, including unemployment, poverty, and population changes. Previous years' lists and criteria for designating these areas are available here . Revitalization or stabilization activities in these geographies are eligible to receive CRA consideration under the community development definition for 12 months after publication of the current list. As with past lists, the agencies apply a one-year lag period for geographies that were included in 2025 but are no longer designated as distressed or underserved in the current list. 2026 List of Distressed or Underserved Nonmetropolitan Middle-Income Geographies (PDF)

The indicative calendar for the Eurosystem’s regular tender operations includes only main refinancing operations (MROs) and three-month longer-term refinancing operations (LTROs). It does not include any supplementary or ad hoc operations which may be carried out in 2028. articles.title: Christine Lagarde: Back to basics in an uncertain environment
articles.extracted_text: It is a pleasure to welcome you to the ECB Forum on Central Banking. Over the past 15 years, the euro area faced an environment of extraordinary pressures that called for unconventional responses. The sovereign debt crisis brought us towards the effective lower bound. The pandemic collapsed demand further. We responded with asset purchases and refinancing operations to support the economy, and new instruments to address fragmentation. Our toolbox expanded in other ways too: forward guidance with lift-off criteria, sequencing commitments, links between different policy instruments. When the environment reversed and Russia cut off our access to natural gas, we responded with a conventional instrument used in unconventional ways: the fastest tightening cycle in our history, raising rates in increments we had never used before. But in today’s environment, monetary policy now finds itself in a different position. We no longer need to reach for unconventional instruments. While we have them at hand, we can now focus on stabilising inflation with policy rates as our primary tool. We no longer need to act with the same force. We can make measured adjustments to rates, calibrated to the shocks we face. And we no longer need complex forms of forward guidance. Our decisions are data-dependent and taken meeting by meeting. Monetary policy has gone back to basics. [ 1 ] Has it? A return to basics does not mean a return to some idealised past, if there is such a thing in monetary policy. As Edmund Burke once wrote, “A state without the means of some change is without the means of its conservation.” [ 2 ] The same holds for monetary policy. Today’s world poses challenges that require us to apply those basics in new ways and to innovate where our existing frameworks fall short. Several factors have made the return to basics possible. Interest rates have moved away from the effective lower bound, driven in part by structural pressures that include rising defence spending. And the shocks of this era are more likely to fall on the supply side than the demand side. Because these negative supply shocks have tended to push prices upwards, interest rates have moved away from the effective lower bound. [ 3 ] But the shift also reflects, perhaps more fundamentally, the policy framework Europe has built in response to those crises. By making the economy more resilient to shocks, this framework has reduced the need for unconventional or forceful policy responses. The ECB’s own instruments have reduced fragmentation risks, including the Transmission Protection Instrument. These tools make it less likely that we observe unwarranted movements in sovereign spreads. They also mean that we can raise rates to address inflation without the concern that tightening itself becomes a source of financial stress. In addition, the strengthening of the European institutional architecture has weakened fragmentation by tackling the bank-sovereign nexus from both sides. European banking supervision and the European resolution framework have made the banking sector more resilient, so that shocks are less easily amplified by the banking system – though we need to remain mindful that financial stability challenges may be building in non-bank financial intermediation, where oversight has not kept pace. European fiscal frameworks have also developed considerably, from the European Stability Mechanism to Next Generation EU and other common borrowing instruments, weakening the nexus from

1.Profits of Industrial Enterprises above the Designated Size from January to May in 2026
articles.extracted_text: From January to May, the total profits of industrial enterprises above the designated size nationwide reached 3,143.96 billion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 18.8% (calculated on a comparable basis, see Annotation 2 for details). From January to May, among industrial enterprises above the designated size, state-holding enterprises realized a total profit of 1,048.66 billion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 19.6%; share-holding enterprises realized a total profit of 2,434.81 billion yuan, an increase of 24.1%; enterprises funded by foreign investors and investors from Hong Kong, Macao and Taiwan realized a total profit of 695.72 billion yuan, an increase of 4.2%; private enterprises realized a total profit of 772.65 billion yuan, an increase of 10.7%. From January to May, the mining industry realized a total profit of 479.52 billion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 33.5%; the manufacturing industry realized a total profit of 2,328.52 billion yuan, an increase of 20.0%; the production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water realized a total profit of 335.92 billion yuan, a decrease of 2.7%. From January to May, the profits of main industries were as follows: the profit of the smelting and pressing of non-ferrous metals increased by 117.1% year on year, that of the manufacture of computers, communication equipment and other electronic equipment increased by 103.9%, that of the manufacture of raw chemical materials and chemical products increased by 71.6%, that of the mining and washing of coal increased by 33.5%, that of the extraction of petroleum and natural gas increased by 17.2%, that of the textile industry increased by 11.7%, that of the manufacture of general-purpose machinery decreased by 0.2%, that of the production and supply of electricity and heat power decreased by 4.1%, that of the manufacture of special-purpose machinery decreased by 5.5%, that of the processing of food from agricultural and sideline products decreased by 13.3%, that of the manufacture of electrical machinery and apparatus decreased by 13.7%, that of the manufacture of automobiles decreased by 19.8%, that of the smelting and pressing of ferrous metals decreased by 37.4%, that of the manufacture of non-metallic mineral products decreased by 48.9%, and the processing of petroleum, coal and other fuels turned from loss to profit. From January to May, industrial enterprises above the designated size achieved a business revenue of 56.55 trillion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 5.5%; the operating costs incurred were 48.04 trillion yuan, an increase of 4.7%; the profit rate of business revenue was 5.56%, a year-on-year increase of 0.63 percentage points. At the end of May, the assets of industrial enterprises above the designated size totaled 193.55 trillion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 5.8%; the liabilities totaled 112.69 trillion yuan, an increase of 6.5%; the ownersâ equity totaled 80.87 trillion yuan, an increase of 5.0%; the asset-liability ratio was 58.2%, a year-on-year increase of 0.3 percentage points. At the end of May, the accounts receivable of industrial enterprises above the designated size totaled 28.17 trillion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 7.7%; the inventory of finished goods was 7.14 trillion yuan, an increase of 8.8%. articles.title: 3.Industrial Production Operation in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 4.5% year on year in real terms (the growth rates of value added are the real growth rates after deducting price factors), 0.4 percentage points higher than the previous month. On a month-on-month basis, in May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 0.40% over the previous month. From January to May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 5.4% year on year. In terms of three sectors, in May, the value added of the mining industry increased by 2.3% year on year, that of the manufacturing industry increased by 4.4%, and that of the production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water increased by 7.6%. In terms of economic types, in May, the value added of state-holding enterprises increased by 3.7% year on year; that of share-holding enterprises increased by 5.2%; that of enterprises funded by foreign investors and investors from Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan increased by 1.9%; and that of private enterprises increased by 2.7%. In terms of industries, in May, the value added of 28 out of 41 major industries continued to show year-on-year growth. Among them, the value added of the mining and washing of coal went up by 3.5%, that of the extraction of petroleum and natural gas up by 1.5%, that of the processing of food from agricultural and sideline products up by 1.5%, that of the manufacture of liquor, beverages and refined tea down by 2.7%, that of the textile industry up by 2.6%, that of the manufacture of raw chemical materials and chemical products up by 0.3%, that of the manufacture of non-metallic mineral products down by 5.6%, that of the smelting and pressing of ferrous metals up by 1.6%, that of the smelting and pressing of non-ferrous metals down by 4.5%, that of the manufacture of general-purpose machinery up by 6.7%, that of the manufacture of special-purpose machinery up by 9.1%, that of the manufacture of automobiles up by 8.3%, that of the manufacture of railway, ship, aerospace and other transport equipment up by 7.4%, that of the manufacture of electrical machinery and apparatus up by 4.7%, that of the manufacture of computers, communication equipment and other electronic equipment up by 17.0%, and that of the production and supply of electricity and heat power up by 8.7%. In terms of products, in May, the output of 300 out of 626 products produced by industrial enterprises above the designated size increased year on year. Among them, the output of rolled steel was 123.03 million tons, down by 2.8% year on year; that of cement was 149.91 million tons, down by 8.1%; that of ten kinds of non-ferrous metals was 6.98 million tons, up by 2.2%; that of ethylene was 3.38 million tons, up by 2.1%; that of motor vehicles was 2.582 million sets, down by 3.2%, including 1.489 million new energy vehicles, up by 17.8%; the electricity generation was 784.3 billion kWh, up by 4.2%; and the crude oil processing volume was 53.72 million tons, down by 9.1%. In May, the product sales ratio of industrial enterprises above the designated size was 96.0%, a year-on-year decrease of 0.1 percentage points; and the export delivery value realized by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 1,388.4 billion yuan, a nominal increase of 10.1% year on year. articles.title: 4.Investment in Fixed Assets from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: From January to May 2026, the national investment in fixed assets (excluding rural households) was 17,851.2 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 4.1% (calculated on a comparable basis, see Annotation 7 for details). Specifically, the investment in intellectual property products increased by 9.3% year on year. By industry, the investment in the primary industry was 388.7 billion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 5.9%; the investment in the secondary industry was 6,498.9 billion yuan, up 0.1%; and the investment in the tertiary industry was 10,963.6 billion yuan, down 6.8%. The industrial investment increased by 0.1% year on year. Specifically, the investment in mining increased by 6.2%, the investment in manufacturing decreased by 0.4%, and the investment in production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water decreased by 0.1%. The investment in infrastructure (see Annotation 1 for details of statistical scope) increased by 0.6% year on year. Specifically, the investment in waterway transportation increased by 23.3%, the investment in civil aviation transportation increased by 21.7%, the investment in ecological conservation and environmental governance increased by 3.5%, and the investment in the production and supply of gas increased by 2.8%. By region, the investment in the eastern region decreased by 6.4% year on year, the investment in the central region decreased by 2.9%, the investment in the western region decreased by 6.6%, and the investment in the northeastern region decreased by 17.5%. By statistical category of registration, the investment in fixed assets of domestic-invested enterprises decreased by 3.9% year on year, the investment in fixed assets of enterprises funded by investors from Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan decreased by 8.7%, and the investment in fixed assets of foreign-invested enterprises decreased by 4.3%. From January to May 2026, non-governmental investment in fixed assets decreased by 7.1% year on year. On a month-on-month basis, the investment in fixed assets (excluding rural households) decreased by 1.91% in May. articles.title: 5.Investment in Real Estate Development from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: I. The Completion of Investment in Real Estate Development From January to May, the investment in real estate development was 3,035.6 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 16.2% (calculated on a comparable basis, see Annotation 5 for details), of which the investment in residential buildings was 2,342.6 billion yuan, down 15.6%. From January to May, the floor space of buildings under construction by real estate development enterprises was 5,487.75 million square meters, a year-on-year decrease of 12.3%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings under construction was 3,808.30 million square meters, down 12.6%. The floor space of buildings newly started was 179.29 million square meters, down 22.6%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings newly started was 130.84 million square meters, down 23.4%. The floor space of buildings completed was 140.87 million square meters, down 23.4%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings completed was 99.99 million square meters, down 25.0%. II. Sales of Newly Built Commercial Buildings From January to May, the floor space of newly built commercial buildings sold was 313.20 million square meters, a year-on-year decrease of 10.8%, of which the floor space of residential buildings sold decreased by 12.1%. The sales of newly built commercial buildings were 2,936.6 billion yuan, down 13.5%, of which the sales of residential buildings decreased by 14.1%. At the end of May, the floor space of commercial buildings for sale was 771.82 million square meters, down 0.4% year on year. Specifically, the floor space for sale held for less than three years was 571.52 million square meters, down 2.8%. III. Funds for Investment This Year for Real Estate Development Enterprises From January to May, the funds for investment this year for real estate development enterprises were 3,275.6 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 19.0%. Among them, domestic loans were 487.5 billion yuan, down 28.7%; self-raised funds were 1,198.5 billion yuan, down 13.0%; deposits and advance receipts were 1,001.2 billion yuan, down 16.1%; individual mortgage loans were 406.6 billion yuan, down 28.0%. articles.title: 6.Total Retail Sales of Consumer Goods from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: From January to May, the total retail sales of consumer goods reached 20,603.1 billion yuan, up by 1.4% year on year. Specifically, the retail sales of consumer goods excluding automobiles reached 19,002.2 billion yuan, up by 2.7%. In May, the total retail sales of consumer goods reached 4,109.0 billion yuan, down by 0.6% year on year. Specifically, the retail sales of consumer goods excluding automobiles reached 3,778.1 billion yuan, up by 1.1%. By business area, from January to May, the retail sales of consumer goods in urban areas reached 17,866.2 billion yuan, up by 1.2% year on year; those in rural areas were 2,736.9 billion yuan, up by 2.6%. In May, the retail sales of consumer goods in urban areas reached 3,574.1 billion yuan, down by 0.9% year on year; those in rural areas were 534.9 billion yuan, up by 1.5%. By consumption pattern, from January to May, the retail sales of goods were 18,254.3 billion yuan, up by 1.2% year on year; the income of the catering industry was 2,348.8 billion yuan, up by 3.1%. In May, the retail sales of goods were 3,648.5 billion yuan, down by 0.7% year on year; the income of the catering industry was 460.5 billion yuan, up by 0.6%. By retail format, from January to May, the retail sales of convenience stores and supermarkets among retail enterprises (units) above the designated size increased by 6.8% and 3.6% year on year, respectively; those of specialty stores, department stores and brand-exclusive stores decreased by 1.2%, 1.8% and 7.6%, respectively. From January to May, Chinaâs online retail sales of goods and services reached 8,317.7 billion yuan, up by 5.9% year on year. Specifically, the online retail sales of goods were 5,271.8 billion yuan, up by 5.0%; among the online retail sales of goods, those of food, clothing, and daily necessities increased by 15.5%, 7.2%, and 1.6%, respectively. The online retail sales of services were 3,045.9 billion yuan, up by 7.6%. articles.title: 7.Energy Production in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May 2026, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size remained at a relatively high level, the growth of crude oil production was steady, the growth of natural gas production fell slightly, and the growth of electricity generation accelerated. I. Raw Coal, Crude Oil and Natural Gas Production and Related Information Raw coal production remained at a relatively high level. In May, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 400 million tons, down by 1.7% year on year; the average daily output was 12.81 million tons. From January to May, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 1.98 billion tons, a year-on-year decrease of 0.3%. The growth of crude oil production was steady. In May, the crude oil production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 18.57 million tons, a year-on-year increase of 0.5%, 0.7 percentage points lower than that in April; the average daily output was 599 thousand tons. From January to May, the crude oil production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 91.31 million tons, a year-on-year increase of 1.1%. The decline in the processing volume of crude oil expanded. In May, the processing volume of crude oil by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 53.72 million tons, a year-on-year decrease of 9.1%, with the decline expanded by 3.3 percentage points from April; the average daily processing volume was 1.733 million tons. From January to May, the processing volume of crude oil by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 292.80 million tons, a year-on-year decrease of 2.2%. Natural gas production fell slightly. In May, the production of natural gas by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 21.7 billion cubic meters, a year-on-year decrease of 2.2%, compared with a 1.9% increase in April; the average daily output was 700 million cubic meters. From January to May, the production of natural gas by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 111.7 billion cubic meters, a year-on-year increase of 1.7%. Electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size grew faster. In May, electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 784.3 billion kWh, a year-on-year increase of 4.2%, with the growth rate 1.6 percentage points higher than that in April; the average daily electricity generation was 25.30 billion kWh. From January to May, electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 3,912.9 billion kWh, a year-on-year increase of 3.6%. By category, in May, the growth of thermal power generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size slowed down, while that of hydropower and solar power generation accelerated; the growth of nuclear power and wind power generation turned from decline to growth. Among them, thermal power generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 2.1% year on year, with the growth rate 1.0 percentage point lower than that in April; hydropower generation increased by 13.0%, with the growth rate 0.8 percentage points higher than that in April; nuclear power generation increased by 5.0%, compared with an 8.7% decline in April; wind power generation increased by 0.5%, compared with a 5.0% decline in April; solar power generation increased by 12.1%, with the growth rate 5.0 percentage points higher than that i

articles.title: 8.National Economy Showed Steady Momentum with Innovation-led and High-quality Developmen...
articles.extracted_text: In May, under the strong leadership of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China (CPC) with Comrade Xi Jinping at its core, all regions and departments fully implemented the decisions and arrangements made by the CPC Central Committee and the State Council, adhered to the general principle of pursuing progress while ensuring stability, fully and faithfully applied the new development philosophy on all fronts, moved faster to foster a new pattern of development, strictly implemented more proactive and effective macro policies, and effectively addressed external shocks and challenges. As a result, production and supply registered steady progress, employment and prices were generally stable, foreign trade demonstrated strong resilience, and new growth drivers witnessed robust development. The national economy maintained steady momentum with innovation-led and high-quality development. 1. Industrial Production Witnessed Accelerated Growth and Equipment Manufacturing and High-Tech Manufacturing Grew Fast. In May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size grew by 4.5 percent year on year, 0.4 percentage points faster than that of the previous month; the month-on-month growth was 0.40 percent. In terms of sectors, the value added of mining increased by 2.3 percent year on year, that of manufacturing grew by 4.4 percent, and that of production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water went up by 7.6 percent. The value added of equipment manufacturing increased by 9.5 percent year on year and that of high-tech manufacturing increased by 15.1 percent, 1.2 percentage points and 2.3 percentage points faster than that of the previous month respectively. An analysis by types of ownership showed that the value added of state holding enterprises was up by 3.7 percent year on year; that of share-holding enterprises was up by 5.2 percent; that of enterprises funded by foreign investors or investors from Hong Kong, Macao and Taiwan was up by 1.9 percent; and that of private enterprises was up by 2.7 percent. In terms of products, the production of 3D printing devices, lithium-ion batteries and industrial robots grew by 54.4 percent, 40.0 percent and 27.9 percent year on year respectively. In the first five months, the value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size went up by 5.4 percent year on year. In May, the Manufacturing Purchasing Managersâ Index stood at 50.0 percent. The Production and Operation Expectation Index was 53.9 percent. In the first four months, the total profits made by industrial enterprises above the designated size were 2,435.8 billion yuan, up by 18.2 percent year on year. 2. Service Sector Grew Steadily and Modern Services Showed Good Momentum of Development. In May, the Index of Services Production grew by 4.4 percent year on year, 0.1 percentage points faster than that of the previous month. Specifically, that of information transmission, software and information technology services, leasing and business services, finance, and transport, storage and postal services grew by 11.3 percent, 10.9 percent, 7.0 percent and 4.8 percent year on year respectively. In the first five months, the Index of Services Production increased by 4.8 percent year on year. In the first four months, the business revenue of service enterprises above the designated size grew by 6.4 percent year on year. In May, the Business Activity Index for Services stood at 50.3 percent and the

articles.extracted_text: Sales Price Indices of Newly Constructed Commercial Residential Buildings in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Sales Price Indices of Second-Hand Residential Buildings in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Price Indices of Newly Constructed Commercial Residential Buildings by Floor Space in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Price Indices of Second-Hand Residential Buildings by Floor Space in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities 1. Survey Coverage: The survey was conducted in the municipal districts of 70 medium and large-sized cities, excluding the counties. 2. Survey Methods: The survey of newly constructed commercial residential buildingsâ sales prices is an overall survey, with basic data derived from online contract-signing data provided by local real estate management departments. The survey of second-hand residential buildingsâ sales prices combines the survey of main units with typical survey, with basic data sourced from reports submitted by real estate agencies or housing service platforms, as well as on-site price collection by surveyors. 3. The calculation of the price indices can be seen in the Statistical Reporting System on Real Estate Prices. 4. If there is no deal record this month, the overall price level is regarded as unchanged. 5. According to the requirements of the statistical system, starting from January 2026, the statistical survey of real estate prices in 70 medium and large-sized cities takes 2025 as the base period for a new round of comparison. The weights of basic categories for each city have been adjusted to better reflect changes in the residential building sales structure. According to the calculation, the impact of the base period rotation on the monthly year-on-year sales price indices of commercial residential buildings in 70 medium and large-sized cities is within 0.03 percentage points on average.

articles.title: 11.Consumer Price Index in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May 2026, Chinaâs Consumer Price Index (CPI) increased by 1.2% year on year. Specifically, the price index in urban areas increased by 1.3% while that in rural areas increased by 1.1%; the price index for food decreased by 1.7% while that for non-food increased by 1.9%; the price index for consumer goods increased by 1.6% while that for services increased by 0.8%. From January to May, on average, Chinaâs CPI increased by 1.0% year on year. In May, Chinaâs CPI decreased by 0.1% month on month. Specifically, the price index in urban areas decreased by 0.1% while that in rural areas decreased by 0.1%; the price index for food decreased by 0.4% while that for non-food decreased by 0.1%; the price index for consumer goods decreased by 0.2% while that for services decreased by 0.1%. I. Year-on-Year Changes in Price Indexes for Various Goods and Services In May, the price index for food, tobacco, liquor and dining out decreased by 0.9% year on year, bringing the CPI down by about 0.26 percentage points. Among food, the price index for meat of livestock decreased by 7.4%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.31 percentage points, of which the price index for pork decreased by 16.1%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.31 percentage points; that for fresh fruits decreased by 2.2%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.04 percentage points; that for eggs increased by 6.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.03 percentage points; that for fresh vegetables increased by 1.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.02 percentage points; that for aquatic products increased by 0.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.01 percentage points. Among the price indexes for the other seven major categories, six increased, and one decreased year on year. Specifically, the price indexes for miscellaneous goods and services, transportation and telecommunication, and health care and medical services increased by 9.9%, 5.4% and 2.1%, respectively; the price indexes for household facilities, articles and services, clothing and education, culture and recreation increased by 1.8%, 1.4% and 1.3%, respectively; the price index for residence decreased by 0.2%. II. Month-on-Month Changes in Price Indexes for Various Goods and Services In May, the price index for food, tobacco, liquor and dining out decreased by 0.2% month on month, bringing the CPI down by about 0.06 percentage points. Among food, the price index for fresh vegetables decreased by 3.6%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.06 percentage points; that for meat of livestock decreased by 0.8%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.03 percentage points, of which the price index for pork decreased by 1.6%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.03 percentage points; that for eggs increased by 5.0%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.03 percentage points; and that for fresh fruits increased by 0.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.01 percentage points. Among the price indexes for the other seven major categories, one increased, four decreased and two remained flat month on month. Specifically, the price index for clothing increased by 0.6%; the price indexes for education, culture and recreation, and health care and medical services remained flat; the price indexes for household facilities, articles and services and miscellaneous goods and services both decreased by 0.4%; the price indexes for transportation and telecommunication and residence decreased by 0.3% and 0.1%, respectively. articles.title: Inside Le Lappe – Remy Renzullo’s first hotel
articles.raw_summary: The interiors wunderkind has turned his eye to a ‘wonderful blank spot’ in Tuscany – we get the first look
articles.extracted_text: Roula Khalaf, Editor of the FT, selects her favourite stories in this weekly newsletter. In Tuscany, a region that exceeds its quota of highly photogenic market towns, few people plump for the charms of Montevarchi. Its centre is flat and a bit faded; two moderately pretty churches and one historical-ish high street its only calling cards. Its well-to-do days are a good 80 years behind it. Bestriding the dioceses of Fiesole and Arezzo, about 30 miles south of Florence, Montevarchi and the hills to the west of it somehow don’t feel part of any of them. Mostly it’s where you exit the autostrada to wind along the 408 regional road, passing through dense woods for half an hour to reach the more famous (and fetching) Chianti. Remy Renzullo has high hopes for these parts. The American interior designer, best known for his recent renovation of Castle Howard in North Yorkshire, has a partner, the architect Niccolò Calabrese de Feo, who grew up here. And, as a result, his affections towards this curious patch of barely tamed woodland have grown. Le Lappe is an 11-bedroom hotel that de Feo’s mother Lorenza opened in 2010 after she had spent years restoring it. She asked Renzullo and de Feo to come on board two years ago, bringing their talents as designer and architect to its total reinvention. The hope is to make it, and the surrounding area – “a bit of a wonderful blank spot” on the tourist map, as Renzullo describes it – a reference point for a new, simpler sort of Tuscan villeggiatura . Le Lappe is made up of two houses: one built on the ruins of a medieval tower, the other a convent rambling across two storeys. De Feo recalls countless childhood walks through the woods from his mother’s villa to Le Lappe, which evolved from a mud-pit building site to an antique-filled casa d’epoca as the years passed. “That was a lot of my weekends,” he says. “I’ve seen this place with no roof, with the first furniture, when the winter garden went in… I have a vivid memory of the opening lunch, with my mother’s dogs running everywhere like they still do today.” Taking it back to brass tacks didn’t faze him; “those scenes were already familiar”. Their youth notwithstanding, Renzullo and de Feo – 34 and 29, respectively – are already aficionados of many things: houses, history, design, society. But inn-keeping is new territory. “Not unlike my career as a decorator, it kind of happened by accident,” says Renzullo. “I fell in love with this place. Niccolò’s mother had done a lovely job with it, but we saw what it had the potential to be. And we formed a vision of how to make it. Beyond that, I’ve always had a romantic attachment to country inns.” Raised in rural New England, with one half of his family roving-bohemian and the other high-Mayflower-Wasp, Renzullo shuttled between houses in Connecticut, coastal Maine and Santa Fe. “When I finally started staying [in hotels], it was a real novelty, very glamorous.” One formative childhood memory was a chance sleep at The Inn of the Anasazi, Santa Fe’s crown jewel. “I remember climbing the bed ladder into this enormous four-poster, and suddenly understanding how meaningful real hospitality – that sense of welcome and caring – could be. “The waiter with the perfect silver tea tray, the honesty bar, someone who really thought about the linens or the menu… I have this foundational idea of these old-school places where you were innately at home but also unmistakably in a hotel.” At Le Lappe, each house has its reading ro

articles.title: Meeting with Kursk Region Governor Alexander Khinshtein
articles.raw_summary: The Governor reported to the President on the region’s socioeconomic development and its efforts to help people who suffered from the Ukrainian Armed Forces.
articles.extracted_text: All content on this site is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International The Governor reported to the President on the region’s socioeconomic development and its efforts to help people who suffered from the Ukrainian Armed Forces. The Governor reported to the President on the region’s socioeconomic development and its efforts to help people who suffered from the Ukrainian Armed Forces. Vladimir Putin started the meeting by saying that industrial output has been increasing in the region, while investment has also been demonstrating positive momentum. Alexander Khinshtein Khinshtein Alexander Governor of Kursk Region weighed in to confirm these trends despite the challenging environment and also considering that some indicators decreased. Still, there have also been some clear positive trends. The region’s investment appeal has been improving with investment almost reaching 55 billion rubles. It signed 27 agreements worth over 87 billion rubles at this year’s St Petersburg Economic Forum. The Governor mentioned the project by Sodruzhestvo to launch in late 2025 a phase three production facility at its complex which processes oil-bearing plants with the view to have an annual output of three million tonnes of oil. This makes this facility without any exaggeration the world’s biggest producer of this kind. It was also in late 2025 that AgroTerra launched the country’s most advanced seed production plant with the capability of covering the demand in the region for soy, corn and wheat. At the same time, the Governor reiterated that the situation remained quite challenging, and the region needed support from the President and the federal Government. Alexander Khinshtein noted that the Kursk Region is an agricultural region, and has been able to demonstrate solid performance in this sector despite the fact that some of the most fertile plots cannot be used for agricultural production so far. Last year, the region produced 4.2 million tonnes of grain, and 4.3 million tonnes of sugar beets. The region consistently ranks second in Russia in terms of pork production, and is the country’s tenth biggest agricultural exporter. The Governor reminded the President that he had already submitted a request for supporting agricultural producers along the border who have incurred damage. This effort is nearing completion, just as instructed by the President. Last year, allocations for developing the sector were equal to 6.7 billion rubles, including 2.5 billion in targeted support for the victims. In his remarks, Alexander Khinshtein pointed out that this robust performance primarily resulted from the selfless and heroic way agricultural producers did their hard work. It is not uncommon for people to put their lives in harm’s way when working in the fields for the sake of ensuring national food security. There is not a single sector within the regional economy where people did not demonstrate that they were up to the task, the Governor said. He went on to say that his key mission consisted of ensuring that the economy keeps working in an uninterrupted manner, no matter what. In his view, creating a free economic zone could be a major asset. The region has almost completed the initiative to include the Kursk Region’s entire territory into a single special economic zone. There is already a list of potential investors and seven major projects with total investment exceeding 245 billion rubles. The region offers substantial advantages, including

articles.title: Greetings to participants and guests of 2nd Forum of the Women of the North
articles.raw_summary: The President sent greetings to the participants and guests of the 2 nd Forum of the Women of the North, which is being held in Moscow as part of the Year of Unity of the Peoples of Russia.
articles.extracted_text: All content on this site is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International The President sent greetings to the participants and guests of the 2 nd Forum of the Women of the North, which is being held in Moscow as part of the Year of Unity of the Peoples of Russia. “You have come to Moscow from across Russia to discuss a wide range of issues affecting the country’s northern regions. These include improving living standards and quality of life, ensuring environmental protection, and preserving the unique cultures, traditions, and customs of the indigenous peoples of the North. Women make an invaluable contribution to the development of Russia’s northern territories. We are proud that, in 2025, a Russian woman became the first in the world to captain a nuclear-powered icebreaker. Women work across key sectors of the economy, make important scientific discoveries, achieve success in the arts, devote considerable effort to community and volunteer initiatives, and provide comprehensive support to participants and veterans of the special military operation, as well as their families. I am confident that your forum will produce meaningful discussions, and that its proposals and recommendations will be considered and put into practice. In this regard, I would also like to recognise the extensive work of the Council of the Eurasian Women’s Forum under the Federation Council, whose initiatives, programmes, and events encourage women to play an active role in the life of our country and society.”
